# Linearity


Core definitions and structural properties of linear systems.

## Linear Systems

A linear system encodes multiple equations into a single matrix-vector form.


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Definition: Linear System
The matrix-vector form of a linear system is **$\bA\bx = \bb$**, where:

- $\bA \in \fR^{m \times n}$ is the coefficient matrix.
  
- $\bx \in \fR^n$ is the vector of unknowns.
  
- $\bb \in \fR^m$ is the right-hand side.

:::


::: {.callout-warning icon=false}
## Exercise

1. Convert $\{x + 2y = 3,\; 3x - y = 1\}$ to $\bA\bx = \bb$.
    
1. Solve in NumPy via `np.linalg.solve(A, b)`. Verify with `np.linalg.norm(b - A @ x)`.

:::


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Theorem: Superposition Principle
If $\bA\bx_1 = \bb_1$ and $\bA\bx_2 = \bb_2$, then for any scalars $\alpha, \beta$:
$$\bA(\alpha\bx_1 + \beta\bx_2) = \alpha\bb_1 + \beta\bb_2.$$
**Implication:**  Solutions to $\bA\bx = \bb$ can be decomposed and recombined linearly.
:::


::: {.callout-warning icon=false}
## Exercise

1. Solve $\bA\bx = 3\bb_1 - 2\bb_2$ given only the solutions $\bx_1, \bx_2$ for $\bb_1, \bb_2$.
    
1. How does `np.linalg.solve(A, B)` for a matrix $\bB$ exploit this?

:::


## Invertibility and Existence

For square systems ($\bA \in \fR^{n \times n}$), a unique solution exists iff $\bA$ is nonsingular.


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Theorem: Equivalences for Invertibility
The following are equivalent for $\bA \in \fR^{n \times n}$:

1. $\bA$ is **invertible** ($\det(\bA) \neq 0$).
    
1. $\bA\bx = \bb$ has a **unique solution** for every $\bb$.
    
1. $\bA\bx = \bzero$ has only the **trivial solution** ($\bx = \bzero$).
    
1. $\text{rank}(\bA) = n$ (Full Rank).
    
1. Columns of $\bA$ are **linearly independent**.

:::


::: {.callout-warning icon=false}
## Exercise

1. Determine invertibility: $\bA = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ 4 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$, $\bB = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$.
    
1. Check consistency of $\bA = \begin{pmatrix}1&2\\3&6\end{pmatrix}, \bb = \begin{pmatrix}1\\4\end{pmatrix}$ via `np.linalg.matrix\_rank`.

:::


## Linear Combinations and Bases


::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
**The Column Perspective.** 
The product $\bA\bx$ is a **linear combination of the columns of $\bA$** weighted by the entries of $\bx$:
$$\bA\bx = x_1 \ba_1 + x_2 \ba_2 + ... + x_n \ba_n.$$
The system $\bA\bx = \bb$ asks: ``Can $\bb$ be represented as a combination of $\bA$'s columns?''
:::


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Definition: Linear Independence
Vectors $\{\bx_1, ..., \bx_k\}$ are independent if $\sum \alpha_i \bx_i = \bzero \Rightarrow \alpha_i = 0$ for all $i$.
:::


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Definition: Basis
A linearly independent set that spans the space. The number of vectors in any basis is the **dimension**.
:::


## Rank

Measures the number of independent directions (dimension of the column space).


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Definition: Rank
$\text{rank}(\bA) \leq \min(m, n)$.

- **Full Rank:** $\text{rank}(\bA) = \min(m, n)$.
    
- **Singular:** Square matrix with $\text{rank}(\bA) < n$.

:::


::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
**Numerical Rank.**  In code, never use row-reduction to find rank; it is numerically unstable. Use `np.linalg.matrix\_rank`, which uses the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD).
:::


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Theorem: Rank-Nullity
For $\bA \in \fR^{m \times n}$: $\text{rank}(\bA) + \text{nullity}(\bA) = n$.

- **Nullity:** Dimension of the null space $\{\bx : \bA\bx = \bzero\}$.

:::


::: {.callout-warning icon=false}
## Exercise

1. Find the general solution to a $2 \times 3$ system as $\bx = \bx_p + \bx_n$ (particular + null space).
    
1. Why must a ``wide'' matrix ($m < n$) have a non-trivial null space?

:::


## The Four Fundamental Subspaces

Solution structure is characterized by four spaces defined by $\bA \in \fR^{m \times n}$ with rank $r$:

1. \textbf{Column Space ($\mathcal{R}(\bA)$):} $\fR^m, \dim=r$. Span of columns.
    
1. \textbf{Null Space ($\mathcal{N}(\bA)$):} $\fR^n, \dim=n-r$. Solutions to $\bA\bx = \bzero$.
    
1. \textbf{Row Space ($\mathcal{R}(\bA^T)$):} $\fR^n, \dim=r$. Span of rows.
    
1. \textbf{Left Null Space ($\mathcal{N}(\bA^T)$):} $\fR^m, \dim=m-r$. Solutions to $\bA^T\by = \bzero$.


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Theorem: Orthogonal Complements

- $\mathcal{R}(\bA^T) \perp \mathcal{N}(\bA)$ in $\fR^n$.
    
- $\mathcal{R}(\bA) \perp \mathcal{N}(\bA^T)$ in $\fR^m$.

**Solvability:**  $\bA\bx = \bb$ has a solution iff $\bb \in \mathcal{R}(\bA)$, i.e., $\bb \perp \mathcal{N}(\bA^T)$.
:::


::: {.callout-warning icon=false}
## Exercise

1. For $\bA = \begin{pmatrix}1&2&3\\2&4&6\end{pmatrix}$, find dimensions and bases for all four spaces.
    
1. Verify $\bb = (1, 3)^T$ is not in the column space. What is the projection of $\bb$ onto $\mathcal{R}(\bA)$?

:::
